text:
NHL



MANALAPAN, Fla. — If you’re a hockey fan, you know “optional” morning skates or practices are often “optional” for some and not so much “optional” for others.

Well, the same thing could be said for the upcoming World Championship in Czechia.

Yes, the competition is indeed voluntary if your nation comes calling, but if you’re an American NHLer whose team missed the playoffs and Bill Guerin dials your number, you may want to strongly consider signing on the dotted line and wearing the red, white and blue sweater — at least if you have any desire to take part in the 2025 4 Nations Face-Off or the 2026 Winter Olympics in Milan, Italy.

Advertisement

“I think it’s important for guys to go,” Guerin, the United States’ general manager for both the 4 Nations Face-Off and theOlympics, said during a sitdown withThe Athleticat last week’sNHLGMs meetings in South Florida. “Hey look, there’s some guys that are going to be a lock for both teams. But there are going to be guys that are on the bubble. If they haven’t played in the playoffs or meaningful games in a long time — or,ever— I want to see how they perform.

“So, in that respect, I think the world championships are extremely important.”

It’s not difficult to determine what bubble players Guerin has in mind. The list may include defensemen likeZach WerenskiandSeth Jonesand forwards likeClayton Keller,Troy Terry,Trevor Zegras,Tage Thompson,Alex Tuchand even Guerin’s ownMinnesota Wildplayer,Matt Boldy.

Boldy has been a key contributor on gold medal-winning United States teams, like the 2021 World Junior Championship team, but Guerin has been publicly critical of his first two postseasons with the Wild. So using him as an example, if he wants to be tapped for either of the upcoming best-on-best tournaments and the Wild miss the playoffs, he may want to strongly consider putting off his summer golf by a month.

Guerin says this isn’t so much an edict as a suggestion.

“I want to see commitment, and if a trip to the Caribbean is more important than the world championships, then you’re not really committed,” Guerin said. “Our player pool is pretty substantial now. I’m not going to say, black and white, ‘If you do this, then yes. If you do this, then no.’ But we’re trying to put the best team on the ice that we possibly can. But we also want guys that are committed.

“I get there are situations, like guys are getting married, guys are having babies, guys are injured. I get that. But if you’re not?”

Advertisement

If you’re not, seeya in Ostrava and Prague, Czechia, from May 10 to 16 as the United States strives to win its second World Championship ever and first since1933.

Guerin himself represented the United States in the Olympics three times as a player (1998 in Nagano, 2002 in Salt Lake City and 2006 in Torino), winning a silver medal in 2002. He also took part in two World Junior Championships and two World Cups, winning gold in 1996. The now-53-year-old USA Hockey Hall of Famer scored 429 goals in the NHL, the ninth-most in NHL history for U.S.-born players.

What he’s saying is not out of the norm.

Don’t think for a second Team Canada won’t be making the same “suggestions” to Canadian NHLers who don’t make the playoffs or are bounced in the first round, which is set to open April 20 and go through around May 4.

In advance of Olympic years, the world championships have always allowed for a bit of leverage for management in choosing subsequent teams. When Wayne Gretzky served as executive director for Canada’s Olympic team in Salt Lake City, he loudly advised players to take part in the 2001 worlds. Lou Nanne, part of the management team for several USA international tournaments, said he used to “demand” the same.

It’s just one of those things that is understood.

“If you go, you get an opportunity to play in front of scouts and management people,” saidSt. Louis BluesGM Doug Armstrong, who was recently named Canada’s Olympics GM and will advise Rick Nash in his GM role for the World Championship and help select Canada’s 4 Nations Face-Off management team. “They’re not tied together that if you don’t go you’re off a list — there’s nothing like that — but the reality is if someone’s going to go there and someone’s going to play well, someone’s going to get somebody’s attention.”

Advertisement

Nash, the formerColumbus Blue Jacketscaptain, said two years ago when he was assistant GM for Canada’s World Championship team, “It almost became that you were begging players to go, and I already feel like this time is different. From talking with Doug Armstrong and (Hockey Canada vice president of hockey operations) Scott Salmond, this is the start of the process over the next few years, with this, the 4 Nations Face-off and the Olympics.

“The international game is a little different. It’s not exactly like the NHL. Different systems, different styles of play. It’s important for guys to come get the experience in the worlds and have success to get the best opportunity to make the next step, whether it’s 4 Nations or the Olympics.”

In other words, Nash and Armstrong are offering a hint a little softer than Guerin’s blunter one — but if someone likeMontreal CanadienscaptainNick Suzukiwants to play in the 4 Nations Face-off or the 2026 Olympics, he should still probably be in Prague this May.

This especially makes sense when you consider how torturous it will be to select a roster ahead of next year’s Feb. 12 to 20 4 Nations Face-Off, which will feature the U.S., Canada, Sweden and Finland. The event will feature 23-player rosters, with three of those players being goaltenders. That means each team can only have 20 skaters — or one extra forward and one extra defenseman. Once the tournament starts, if you sustain multiple injuries, there’s no reserve list or taxi squad to choose from.

So Guerin especially wants bubble players who miss this year’s Stanley Cup playoffs to attend the World Championship so the management team, which is led by GM Brett Peterson (Florida Panthersassistant GM), can ascertain which players could fill roles they’re otherwise not accustomed to.

Most players in the upcoming tournaments play top-six and top power-play roles on their NHL teams. But that may not be the case on talent-rich rosters in the 4 Nations Face-Off and Olympics.The Athleticprojected what the U.S. roster could look likeunder Guerin last month, for a sense of its depth.



Guerin plans to be at the World Championship to begin the evaluation process for the upcoming tournaments.

Advertisement

“These are short tournaments,” Guerin said. “Almost everybody that’ll be on the teams is a power-play guy or a penalty-kill guy or a first-line center, winger, top-pair defenseman. Almost everybody on the teams are star players. So we want to see, ‘Can you buy in? Can you be happy with less ice time than you normally get? Will you be supportive of the guys that are on the power play if you’re not?’

“We need good guys that are going to be good teammates and guys that will fall in and be willing to accept roles that may be uncomfortable. To me, that’s so important. That’s an important reason for guys to do these world championships year in and year out — not just this year when it’s a 4 Nations year or an Olympic year — so we know who you are.

“It’s not just the hockey part. It’s that we get to know you a little bit because, to me, the buy-in factor is so enormous. It’s not just skating, shooting and passing. It’s the heart. It’s the emotion. It’s what you’re willing to do for the team.”

The first six players for each country in the 4 Nations Face-Off must be announced by the end of June. There won’t be any practices until Feb. 10-11 before the tournament starts in one American city and one Canadian city Feb. 12.

Guerin has conducted a few meetings already and has checked a few boxes, like selecting his staff, for one.

That’s something USA Hockey is expected to announce next month.

Guerin wouldn’t reveal which shoulders he has tapped, but there’s a great chance thePittsburgh Penguins’Mike Sullivan will be the head honcho behind the bench. Sullivan and Guerin have a long history together from Guerin’s Penguins days, and Sullivan was supposed to coach the United States at the 2022 Olympics until the pandemic caused the NHL to pull out.

“Obviously, we were well down the road with the Olympic team at the time when the rug got pulled out from under us there, so to speak,” Sullivan toldThe Athleticlast month. “So we had gone through a process as a group there. I don’t think that there’s any better guy that’s suited to lead that group with all of the experience that he’s had and international play in the NHL and his experience as a manager than Billy.”

Advertisement

As for Guerin’s management staff,New York RangersGM Chris Drury was supposed to be his assistant GM for the Olympics two years ago. Guerin may have been being cagey, but he said that doesn’t necessarily mean his managerial staff will have the same roles this time around.

He has a large group of American execs he can choose from: Drury,New Jersey DevilsGM Tom Fitzgerald, Panthers GM Bill Zito,San Jose SharksGM Mike Grier,Colorado AvalancheGM Chris MacFarland,Buffalo SabresGM Kevyn Adams,Calgary FlamesGM Craig Conroy and, of course, USA Hockey lifersNew York IslandersGM Lou Lamoriello andCarolina HurricanesGM Don Waddell. It’s likely Wild director of player personnel Chris Kelleher will be on the staff, as well as Peterson.

Guerin said he has notified his staff but hasn’t tapped into them a lot yet because he wants to be respectful of the fact NHL seasons are still going.

“When we do this stuff, I want the focus to be on this,” he said. “It’s hard to ask guys if they’re in the middle of a playoff push to do a USA Hockey call.”

Guerin intends to have the same staff for the 2026 Olympics as he has for the 4 Nations Face-Off, for continuity’s sake.

“I can’t explain how excited I am,” Guerin said. “We’re in it to win it.”

And that process starts in Czechia with the World Championship.

If you’re an NHL player who declines an invitation or sends Guerin or Peterson to voicemail and still wants to be part of the 4 Nations Face-Off and Olympics, well, it probably won’t be forgotten.

GO DEEPER

U.S. Olympic team names Wild's Bill Guerin general manager

Chris Johnston and Aaron Portzline contributed to this story.

(Top photo of Team USA after a loss to Latvia during the 2023 World Championship: Jari Pestelacci / Getty Images)

Michael Russois a senior writer covering the Minnesota Wild and the National Hockey League for The Athletic. He has covered the NHL since 1995 (Florida Panthers) and the Wild since 2005, previously for the South Florida Sun-Sentinel and Minneapolis Star Tribune. Michael is a five-time Minnesota Sportswriter of the Year and in 2017 was named the inaugural Red Fisher Award winner as best beat writer in the NHL. Michael can be seen on NHL Network; and heard on KFAN (100.3 FM) and the Worst Seats in the House podcast (talknorth.com). He can be found on Instagram and X at @russohockey and Bluesky at @russohockey.bsky.social.Follow Michael on Twitter@RussoHockey
title: Bill Guerin’s message for U.S. Olympic and 4 Nations hopefuls: Don’t blow off the world championships - The Athletic
author: Michael Russo
description: Guerin wants see how players fit in at this year's World Championship ahead of the 2025 and 2026 international best-on-best events.
keywords: Minnesota Wild, NHL, Top Sports News
